text: Chronic mechanical loading produces significant increases in tendon stiffness with a moderate-to-large effect size.
Chronic mechanical loading increases tendon Young's modulus (material stiffness) with a moderate-to-large effect size.
Chronic mechanical loading produces only small increases in tendon cross-sectional area, suggesting morphological adaptation is limited compared to material property changes.
Loading magnitude (intensity) is the key driver of tendon stiffness adaptation, significantly more important than the type of muscle contraction used.
Muscle contraction type (concentric, eccentric, isometric) does not significantly affect the magnitude of tendon stiffness adaptation.
Tendon adaptation to loading is driven more by changes in material properties (Young's modulus) than by morphological changes (cross-sectional area).
specific_value: Young's modulus SMD 0.69 vs CSA SMD 0.24

text: Exercise interventions of 12 weeks or longer tend to produce greater tendon stiffness adaptations than shorter interventions, with a minimum of 8 weeks needed for measurable change.
specific_value: ≥12 weeks higher SMD (not statistically significant); minimum ≥8 weeks
